description: A familiar face appears at the food court table every single day. Same table. Same order. There may be more to her routine than habit.

phases.text: A woman in a burgundy cardigan eats lunch at the same food court table every day. She nods politely when she passes your storefront.
She always pauses at the same spot in the concourse — just past the fountain — before turning toward the food court. Every single day.
While waiting for her order, she writes in a small spiral notebook. The cover is worn smooth. This is not a new notebook.
Her name is Odalys. For three years she has been documenting mall store prices for a local consumer advocacy newsletter. Your pricing history appears on twelve of her pages.

steps.narrative_text: The same woman pauses outside your store and checks her notebook.
She glances at your price tag as a customer buys. Something gets written down.

stages.narrative_text: The same woman pauses outside your store and checks her notebook.
She glances at your price tag as a customer buys. Something gets written down.

resolution_text: Odalys introduces herself. She has been writing the Concourse Consumer Digest since 2000. Your store is featured in the next issue as 'a rare honest price on a busy floor.' She hands you a back issue from her bag. Your store isn't in it. You kept your prices fair before anyone noticed.

description: Someone with aviator glasses browses without buying twice a week. The things he says in passing sound oddly familiar from a local review site.

phases.text: A man in aviator glasses and a worn jacket browses your store twice a week. He never buys. He asks specific questions about provenance.
A scathing review of the kiosk three doors down appears on MallTruth dot net. The phrase 'aggressively indifferent pricing' matches something he muttered while walking past.
He photographs your price tag with a small point-and-shoot camera when he thinks you aren't watching. He does this every visit.
He is MallTruth_Watch, the most-read anonymous retail critic in the county. He has been documenting your pricing for eight weeks. His conclusion: you are the control group.

steps.narrative_text: The man with the camera lingers near your clearance shelf.
MallTruth dot net posts: 'One store where the price tag tells the truth.'

stages.narrative_text: The man with the camera lingers near your clearance shelf.
MallTruth dot net posts: 'One store where the price tag tells the truth.'

resolution_text: He slides his press badge across your counter without a word. Reginald Pham, MallTruth_Watch. Eight weeks of data. Forty-seven price checks. 'You're the only one who didn't flinch.' He has already drafted the feature. He asks if he can quote your prices directly. You say yes.

description: Unit 7B has been vacant for eight months. The lights should not be flickering.

phases.text: Unit 7B is dark and gated. A 'For Lease' sign has been in the window since spring. Maintenance says it's fully cleared out.
At closing time, the lights in unit 7B flicker on for three or four seconds, then go dark again. This has happened on at least four separate evenings.
On some mornings, there is a faint but unmistakable smell of drip coffee near the unit's gated entrance. The food court doesn't open for another hour.

Bertram Holt, retired owner of a luggage shop that occupied 7B for eleven years, has been letting himself in with a key the property manager forgot to collect. He brings a thermos and a paperback. He just needed somewhere quiet.

steps.narrative_text: The lights in 7B flicker again at closing. Two seconds this time.
You try the gate at opening time. It swings open. There is a folding chair and an empty thermos.

stages.narrative_text: The lights in 7B flicker again at closing. Two seconds this time.
You try the gate at opening time. It swings open. There is a folding chair and an empty thermos.

resolution_text: Bertram apologizes twice before you can say a word. He offers to leave immediately. You tell him to finish his chapter. He reads for twenty minutes, thanks you formally, and never comes back. You leave the gate unlatched the following morning, just in case.

description: An old man on the bench near the entrance has watched every store in this mall open and close. He may have opened the first one.

phases.text: An elderly man in a cardigan and pressed slacks sits on the bench near the entrance most afternoons. He watches the foot traffic with the patience of someone who has been here a long time.
Two long-running vendors stop to greet him by name. They do not introduce themselves. He waves them off gently and goes back to watching.
The management office has a framed newspaper clipping from 1987 on the wall. The headline is 'New Mall Opens Its Doors.' The man in the photo wears the same cardigan.

His name is Vincent Muro. He opened the original anchor store here on the mall's first day in October 1987. He still owns the lease on an empty unit at the far end of the east corridor, and he has no intention of subletting it. He just likes the light in the afternoon.

steps.narrative_text: He nods as you pass. It is the nod of someone who has seen this before.
He stops at your storefront on his way out and looks at the sign for a long moment. 'Decent name,' he says.

stages.narrative_text: He nods as you pass. It is the nod of someone who has seen this before.
He stops at your storefront on his way out and looks at the sign for a long moment. 'Decent name,' he says.

resolution_text: Vincent sits with you at a small table near the food court. He has a coffee. You have a coffee. He tells you about the day the mall opened — the ribbon cutting, the parking lot, the line that went around the building. He doesn't tell you about the lease at the end of the east corridor. Some things you figure out later, on your own.
